md: "Two negatives make a positive" — students hear it for multiplication, then get told it works for subtraction too: $7 - (-3) = 7 + 3 = 10$.
But *why* does taking away a negative make a number **bigger**? Here are two ways to be sure it must.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: For any numbers $a$ and $b$, $\;a - (-b) = a + b$. Subtracting $-b$ has the exact same effect as adding $b$.

md: Hold the first number at $7$ and subtract a column of shrinking numbers. Each time the number being subtracted drops by $1$, the answer goes **up** by $1$:
The answers climb $4, 5, 6, 7, \ldots$ steadily. To keep that staircase from breaking, the next rows are forced:
Subtracting $-3$ landed on $10$ — exactly where $7 + 3$ lands.
Subtraction has a precise definition: to subtract a number means to **add its opposite**.
Now substitute $x = -b$. The opposite of $-b$ is $+b$ — flipping a sign twice returns you to the start:

On the number line this is the cleanest picture: adding a positive number is a step to the **right**. Subtracting $-b$ reverses the leftward pull of a negative — so it, too, steps right. Try it: set the start, then add a positive amount.

blocks.title: The takeaway
blocks.body: A negative number pulls you left; *removing* that pull lets you go right. "Minus a minus" is plus — not by decree, but because subtraction was defined as adding the opposite. $\blacksquare$
